food_subgroup: Rice-based mixed dishes with meat and vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese pork belly rice with vegetables. FOODON_00002809 (pork dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pork meat dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Burmese-style pork belly rice with vegetables.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this specific Burmese pork belly rice dish. SNOMED CT coverage for Southeast Asian ethnic dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of pork belly rice with vegetables, approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 200 g steamed white rice, 150 g grilled or braised pork belly, and 100 g mixed cooked vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Pork belly, cooked; White rice, cooked; Mixed vegetables, cooked — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Burmese pork belly rice with vegetables as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component proportions: approximately 44% steamed white rice (USDA FDC #168878), 33% cooked pork belly (USDA FDC #168278), and 22% mixed cooked vegetables (USDA FDC #169230), with minor allowance for cooking oil and soy-based seasoning. Per-100g values are derived from these proportions and represent a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice (boiled) and mixed rice-based meal analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese pork belly rice with vegetables. GI of 65 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: boiled white rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database). The presence of pork belly fat and mixed vegetables moderately attenuates the overall glycemic response compared to plain white rice. A composite GI of 65 (medium) is used as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 92.25) / 100 ≈ 60.0 g, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 20.5 × 4.5 = 92.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1395 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced soy sauce, as a single serving may contribute approximately 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 65) driven by white rice; high glycemic load per serving (~60)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious of the high glycemic load per serving; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion and increasing the vegetable component can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~3.5 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~38 mg per 100 g) from pork belly, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its saturated fat and sodium content; choosing leaner pork cuts and requesting less oil during preparation can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Belly Rice with Vegetables (Burmese: အခေါက်ကင်ထမင်းပေါင်း, romanized: a-khaut-kyin htamin paung) is a traditional Burmese rice plate dish consisting of grilled or braised pork belly served over steamed white rice, accompanied by a portion of cooked mixed vegetables. The pork belly is typically marinated in soy sauce, garlic, and ginger before being grilled or slow-braised until tender, yielding a rich, savoury, and slightly caramelised flavour with a moist, fatty texture. The steamed jasmine or long-grain white rice provides a neutral starchy base, while the vegetables — commonly including leafy greens, cabbage, or seasonal produce — add colour, fibre, and micronutrients. This dish is a staple comfort food in Myanmar and is commonly found in Burmese restaurants across Singapore, including at establishments such as Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (~198 kcal per 100 g) due to the fat content of pork belly, and provides moderate protein (~8.5 g per 100 g). The sodium content is elevated (~310 mg per 100 g) due to soy-based seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is medium (~65), driven primarily by the white rice component, and the glycemic load per standard serving is high (~60), which is clinically relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat content from pork belly warrants attention for cardiovascular health. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher dietary practices.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Burmese composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for cooked pork belly, steamed white rice, and mixed cooked vegetables. GI is analogously estimated from white rice GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the component-based estimation approach and the absence of a direct match in any primary regional food composition database.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked pork belly (FDC #168278), cooked white rice (FDC #168878), and mixed cooked vegetables (FDC #169230), weighted at approximately 33%, 44%, and 22% respectively, with a small allowance for cooking oil and soy seasoning. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in the source database; pork belly contains trace vitamin D but the composite value is not determinable from available component data. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting naturally occurring trans fats from pork; this is not an industrially added trans fat. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; the actual composite GI may be lower due to fat and fibre attenuation. 5. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON included 'Noodle' and 'Lor Mee', which appear to be erroneous tags from the field survey platform and have been disregarded; this dish is a rice plate, not a noodle dish. 6. The dish is served at Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant, 77 High Street, Singapore, as sourced from field survey data at SGD 12.00. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component proportions and carry medium uncertainty. 8. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite dish.
